figures.caption: Figure 13: Sequential correspondence between the proposed hierarchical framework and PivotRL. Both optimize a policy over intermediate states, but PivotRL enforces feasibility through verifierbased local rewards, while our method enforces feasibility through projection into a feasible domain. This visualizes the Appendix X equivalences: St ↔ st, oracle supervision↔ expert trajectories, protocol reward↔ verifier reward, and drift detection↔ pivot selection.

Table 11: Effect of dataset size and number of epochs on decision-criteria adaptation for GPT-5 and Mistral-7B_5000_10. Accuracy (Model) reflects correct fidelity selection; Error (Point) reports mean ℓ1 distance (lower is better). Results are reported as mean ± 95% confidence interval over matched MFBO seeds. Time is given as minutes:seconds. “–” stands for non-feasible responses.

Table 13: Effect of dataset size and number of epochs on decision-criteria adaptation for GPT-5-nano and Mistral-7B_5000_10. Accuracy (Model) reflects correct fidelity selection; Error (Point) reports mean ℓ1 distance (lower is better). Results are reported as mean ± 95% confidence interval over matched MFBO seeds. Time is given as minutes:seconds. “–” stands for non-feasible responses.

Table 12: Effect of dataset size and number of epochs on decision-criteria adaptation for GPT-5-nano and Llama-3.1-8B_5000_5. Accuracy (Model) reflects correct fidelity selection; Error (Point) reports mean ℓ1 distance (lower is better). Results are reported as mean ± 95% confidence interval over matched MFBO seeds. Time is given as minutes:seconds.
Figure 8: Training and evaluation loss curves for Table 10.
Figure 11: Training and evaluation loss curves for Table 13.
Figure 9: Training and evaluation loss curves for Table 11.
Figure 10: Training and evaluation loss curves for Table 12.

Table 8: Response of distilled Llama-3.1-8B_5000_5 as a function of prompt length. The fourth column shows the ratio of the prompt size to the theoretical feasible bound (|ρ|feasible ≈ 49,152 tokens), while the fifth column reports the ratio to the Proposition 5.1 saturation estimate (|ρ|propsat ≈ 2.38·103 tokens). “–” indicates an empty response.

Table 9: Response of distilled Mistral-7B_5000_10 as a function of prompt length. The fourth column shows the ratio of the prompt size to the theoretical feasible bound (|ρ|feasible≈61,440 tokens), while the fifth column reports the ratio to the Proposition 5.1 saturation estimate (|ρ|propsat ≈1.03·103 tokens). “–” indicates an empty response and “x” stands for non-protocol response.

Table 10: Effect of dataset size and number of epochs on decision-criteria adaptation for GPT-5 and Llama-3.1-8B_5000_5. Accuracy (Model) reflects correct fidelity selection; Error (Point) reports mean ℓ1 distance (lower is better). Results are reported as mean ± 95% confidence interval over matched MFBO seeds. Time is given as minutes:seconds.
Figure 4: Training and evaluation loss curves for Table 5, (Part 1).
Figure 5: Training and evaluation loss curves for Table 5, (Part 2).

Table 5: Effect of dataset size and number of epochs on the distillation process for Llama-3.1-8B. Time is h:min and 10:00 is the execution limit. The expected transferred data |TT | is computed using Assumption 1 considering δ1 = 0.25 and δ2 = 0.3.

Table 6: Effect of dataset size and number of epochs on the distillation process for Mistral-7B. Time is h:min and 10:00 is the execution limit. The expected transferred data |TT | is computed using Assumption 1 considering δ1 = 0.25 and δ2 = 0.3.
Figure 6: Training and evaluation loss curves for Table 6, (Part 1).

Figure 3: Visualization of the results obtained by each considered model in the ablation study represented in Table 4. The ground-truth model is shown in black, and the approximation models are shown as dashed colored lines: red, yellow, green, and purple correspond to fidelity levels 1, 2, 3, 4, respectively. In blue, the obtained approximation using each model is plotted with its uncertainty area (Part 2).

Table 4: Teacher–student ablation with OpenAI teachers and local students. Results are reported as mean ± 95% confidence interval over matched MFBO seeds. Lower is better for distance to optimal, hierarchical frequency, and cost; higher is better for number of evaluated points.

Figure 12: Illustration of the multi-fidelity Bayesian maximization setup. The black curve denotes the unknown target objective g(x), while the colored curves represent surrogate models of increasing fidelity and cost. Higher-fidelity surrogates provide more accurate approximations at a larger evaluation cost.

Figure 2: Visualization of the results obtained by each considered model in the ablation study represented in Table 4. The ground-truth model is shown in black, and the approximation models are shown as dashed colored lines: red, yellow, green, and purple correspond to fidelity levels 1, 2, 3, 4, respectively. In blue, the obtained approximation using each model is plotted with its uncertainty area.
